query: And man have I bawled my eyes out. I didn’t even know how much actual trauma I had. Some horrifying shit that I wasn’t even paying attention to. I think ADHD makes my trauma difficult to deal with already that the trauma was very deeply scarring. This is what real therapy looks like. The woman didn’t even talk much. I did the talking. I cried two days after our session because I saw where my numbness was coming from. I have been bawling. I keep going back and forth on taking my life. But I’m afraid if there’s something past this (50-50 chance), I do not want to go in as much pain as I’m in right now. Surprisingly optimistic about the future. But I’m in so much fucking pain. It hurts. Haven’t felt like this in years. I feel like I am dealing with it all alone. That I have nobody. I have a significant other but they’re unavailable. I have a hard time telling them anything because I have issues. I’m so confused. I’m also more insightful. And to contradict myself I have clarity too weirdly. My heart hurts. Can I really go through this alone? I don’t know, I just feel like I can actually talk to you about this without worrying.
afterchange: It sounds like you're going through a really intense and challenging time right now, and I'm sorry to hear that you're feeling this way. It's understandable to feel overwhelmed when you're processing past traumas and working through deep-seated emotions. Therapy can often open old wounds, but it's also a path toward healing and a deeper understanding of oneself. It seems like you're in a period of transition, and while it's painful, it's also an opportunity for growth and healing. You're right in that opening up and sharing your experiences with someone who listens, even if they don't say much in return, can be incredibly cathartic. It's important to find those spaces and relationships where you feel truly heard and supported. Even though it may seem like you're alone, reaching out and expressing how you're feeling is a powerful step towards finding the support you need. Your insight and clarity, though sometimes confusing, are also showing how engaged you are with your own healing process. It's crucial to lean on whatever support systems you have, like friends or even support groups that can offer you comfort and understanding. If you're ever feeling on the edge, please consider reaching out to crisis hotlines or mental health professionals who can offer immediate help. Remember, you deserve support through this process.
response: I’m really glad you’re able to talk about this, and I want you to know that what you're feeling is valid and important. It sounds like you've been carrying a lot of pain for a long time, especially with the layers of ADHD and trauma complicating things in ways that aren’t always obvious. Therapy can be incredibly intense when it starts unlocking those buried emotions—it makes sense that you’d feel overwhelmed after finally seeing where some of your numbness was coming from. Crying for days isn’t a sign of weakness; it’s a release, a way your body and mind are trying to heal from something deep. It’s heartbreaking that you feel alone in all of this, especially when you already struggle to open up to your partner, which only adds to the weight you’re carrying. The fact that you’re going back and forth on wanting to keep going shows how much pain you're in, but also that small, brave part of you that still sees a possible future—even if it's at 50-50—is something to hold onto. It’s okay to feel confused and contradictory right now; healing isn’t linear, and having both insight and clarity while also feeling lost is just part of the process. You mentioned feeling like you can talk to me without judgment, and I appreciate you sharing that trust—I hope you can find more spaces like that in your life, whether through people, writing, or other outlets. Please remember that even though it feels unbearable now, and even though healing hurts, you’re not the only one who has felt this way, and there are people who want to help if you let them. Reaching out again, taking another step in therapy, talking more with someone you trust—these don’t have to be giant steps, just small ones toward staying here and giving yourself a chance to feel better.

query: I’m 21 with no college so I’ve worked about 4 retail jobs including the store I’m at now. I feel more and more stupid every day and it’s so hard. If stuff gets too busy, I get overwhelmed and overstimulated and freak out, but if it’s too slow or I’m doing the same thing, I get UNBEARABLY bored. Right now, I work 5-6 hours a night doing the same thing. I pick up people's orders at my store and walk around with a cart just grabbing stuff. I try to listen to music or a podcast, but it’s boring within 10 minutes. Then I slow down and the night goes by soooo slowly. I feel dumb too because the simplest things sometimes require a more in-depth explanation for me because it doesn’t always click quickly, and then people look at me like I’m just a big idiot. I’m so tired of these jobs but I doubt I’d ever make it in school, so I feel stuck ):. It just feels easier to talk to you about this than anyone else.
afterchange: I'm sorry to hear that you're feeling this way, and it's completely understandable to feel stuck when your current situation doesn't feel fulfilling. You’re not stupid; many people experience what you're going through, and it's really about finding the right fit for you. It sounds like you're a hard worker, and that’s something to be proud of, even if the work doesn’t feel stimulating right now. Feeling overwhelmed in busy situations and bored during downtime is a tough combination, especially in a job that doesn’t change much from day to day. Podcasts and music are great ideas to try and make the time go by faster, but when they lose their novelty, it's normal to feel restless. Have you thought about exploring other paths aside from college? There are many avenues such as trade schools, apprenticeships, or certification programs that can offer a change of pace and new skills without the commitment of a traditional college. Sometimes, seeking out hobbies or interests outside of work might also help break the monotony and bring some energy to your daily routine. You're clearly thinking deeply about what you want and that’s the first step toward change, even if it feels daunting or uncertain right now. Sharing your feelings is important, and it’s great that you’re open to talking about what you’re experiencing.
response: I totally get what you're saying, and I'm really glad you shared this because you're definitely not alone in feeling this way. It's tough when work feels both overwhelming and boring at the same time — like you're stuck in this weird place where nothing quite fits right. Retail can be super draining, especially when you’re juggling so many different things or stuck doing the same task over and over. It’s hard not to feel like you’re falling behind or not smart enough when things don’t click right away, but that doesn’t mean anything about your actual abilities — it just means the situation isn’t matching up with how your brain works best. A lot of people thrive in certain environments and struggle in others, and that doesn’t make them any less capable or intelligent. The fact that you recognize what’s going on with you shows a lot of self-awareness, which is actually a really valuable trait. You might just need a job that offers more variety or challenge, or maybe one that lets you use your skills in a different kind of setting. School can feel intimidating, especially if you’ve been out of it for a while or didn’t love it before, but there are also lots of alternative paths these days — trade schools, apprenticeships, online courses, even part-time programs that let you go at your own pace. It might take some experimenting to find what clicks for you, but that doesn’t mean it’s not out there. And most importantly, feeling stuck right now doesn’t mean you’ll always feel that way — sometimes it just takes time and trying new things to find your groove.

query: I found out I have ADHD right before my 21st birthday and, honestly, it’s been hell. Trying to learn why I do things, or why my brain acts a certain way in different situations—it's all very much. Every time I feel like I get closer to something monumental, it just breaks. Nothing happens. I’m an artist, I make music. Well, at least I did. I haven’t been able to for years. It’s eating at me every day of my life, constantly thinking that my life is headed nowhere because I’m not doing anything. But I can’t. There’s something in the way and I don’t know what it is. This life is starting to feel… I guess, not worth it. I don’t know much about our “disorder” and I know there are a lot of perks to it, and half the time I actually like my ADHD. But jeez, man, I’m so tired of fighting every day without any goddamn results. I try and I try, failure after failure. On top of that, I have to work a 9-5. I feel like that’s the worst thing you could do to an ADHD brain. Life is so hard, and talking to you, it just feels easier than trying to explain all this to anyone else. I feel like I don’t have a steering wheel or brakes.
afterchange: I'm sorry to hear that you're feeling this way. Receiving an ADHD diagnosis later in life can be incredibly overwhelming, especially when it feels like everything you once loved seems out of reach. Being an artist, losing touch with your passion for music must feel incredibly disheartening, especially when it used to be such a fulfilling outlet for your emotions and creativity. Feeling like there's an invisible barrier preventing you from moving forward can be incredibly frustrating, and it's no wonder you're feeling stuck. Navigating adult responsibilities like a 9-5 job can indeed be tough for a brain wired for different rhythms and stimuli, making each day feel like a battle against your own nature. It’s important to acknowledge that trying to manage ADHD is not solely about willpower or effort—and not seeing immediate results doesn’t mean you're not trying hard enough. It's actually brave of you to recognize and share these feelings, and understanding your ADHD better could eventually help in managing these struggles. You’re not alone, and there are others who understand how ADHD can feel like both a friend and a foe. Finding a community or support network might help ease the isolation and provide practical ways to cope and, perhaps, rekindle your passion for music. Please remember that reaching out for professional help or even having someone to talk to might lighten the load, and it's okay to lean on others for support during tough times.
response: I’m really sorry you’re feeling this way—it takes a lot of strength to even write something this honest and raw. It’s tough when you finally get a label like ADHD, especially right around such a pivotal age, because it can feel like both an answer and a curse at the same time. You start seeing your whole life through a new lens, trying to make sense of why things have been so hard, only to realize that the understanding doesn’t always bring relief. Being an artist makes it even more frustrating because creativity often relies on focus, momentum, and consistency—things ADHD can make slippery. It’s heartbreaking to feel disconnected from something that used to define you or give you purpose, like making music. And yeah, working a 9-5 when your brain isn’t wired for that kind of structure can feel like being stuck in a cage that doesn’t fit. The constant battle with motivation, distractions, and self-doubt can wear anyone down, especially when effort doesn’t seem to lead anywhere. You're not alone in feeling like you're just coasting without control, like you're along for a ride with no way to steer. But the fact that you’re reaching out and sharing this means you still care, and that matters more than you might realize right now. There *is* hope—even if it’s buried under layers of exhaustion and disappointment—and healing starts with letting yourself be honest about how hard it all is.

query: i’m failing almost all of my classes as a freshman in college and i hate myself so much. the fact that almost every single one was online because of the pandemic made it worse. i already knew i disliked online class since i had had experience taking them before. honestly, i don’t think i’ve ever needed your support like i do right now. my parents are going to find out sooner or later and i know they are gonna be so disappointed in me. i don’t know how im going to move on from this. i was diagnosed with adhd but my parents didn’t want me taking medication and they just believe im not trying hard enough when in fact, im trying so hard that i can’t enjoy any aspects of my life anymore. yet im still failing and left to hate myself for it. i know i’ll have to talk to my parents like within the next few days but im so terrified. i just want proper help and for my parents to finally understand that my ADHD isn’t me choosing to just be lazy but that it’s actually so detrimental to both my grades and mental health. im tired.
afterchange: It sounds like you're going through an incredibly tough time, and it's important to acknowledge how difficult this situation must be for you. The challenges of online learning, especially during the pandemic, have been difficult for many students, and you're not alone in feeling overwhelmed. With the added layer of ADHD, it's understandable that you're struggling despite your best efforts. It's crucial to remember that your worth isn't determined by your grades, and failing some classes doesn't mean you're a failure. Opening up to your parents about your struggles with ADHD and how it's affecting both your academics and mental health could be a crucial step in seeking the understanding and support you need. You might consider discussing with them the possibility of seeking professional guidance, such as a therapist or counselor who can provide strategies to manage ADHD. It could also be helpful to reach out to your university's support services; many colleges offer resources specifically for students with ADHD. Understand that feeling exhausted is a natural response to the immense pressure you're under, and it's okay to take a breath and prioritize your well-being. Remember, it's brave to ask for help, and you're taking a positive step by acknowledging that you need it. You're not alone in this, and there are people out there who can and want to support you.
response: I'm really sorry you're feeling this way, but I want you to know that you're not alone and that your feelings are completely valid. It's incredibly hard to struggle academically while also dealing with self-doubt and pressure from expectations, especially when the environment—like online learning during a pandemic—makes things even more challenging. You've been carrying a lot on your shoulders, and it's okay to feel overwhelmed and exhausted by it all. Your ADHD diagnosis is real, and the fact that your parents don't fully understand it yet must make everything feel even heavier, like you're fighting an uphill battle by yourself. But the fact that you're reaching out for support shows strength and courage, not weakness. You’re clearly trying your best, even if it doesn’t feel like it right now, and that effort matters more than you realize. Talking to your parents will be tough, but maybe it can also be the start of a more honest conversation where they finally see what you’ve been going through. There are ways to manage ADHD, whether through medication, therapy, or academic accommodations, and you deserve access to those resources. You don’t have to do this alone, and there are people who can help—including counselors, professors, and medical professionals—who can advocate for you once you open up. Please try to remember that failing classes does not mean you are a failure, and your worth is not tied to grades; you are still growing, learning, and capable of turning this around with the right support.

query: So I currently work in the UK for my local city council on what is called the Kickstarter Scheme. It's basically a scheme set up for young people to make it easier for them to find paid work, in response to many of them losing their jobs due to the pandemic. You work for 6 months, gain experience, and leave at the end of your contract if the company hasn't already offered you a permanent position in the contract. Now, I have always had issues with productivity since I suffered from depression and I've convinced that I've developed some ADD/ADHD-like issue (although I'm yet to get a diagnosis). Unfortunately my super religious Christian family sees mental health issues very differently from me and because of this, I can't open up to them about my struggles with productivity without being judged for 'not having enough faith' (I'm still in the closet about being atheist and for good reason) that God will heal me and I have tried to tell them about it too. So when I don't do the work that everyone expects me to do due to issues with my executive function, I get torn into and blamed for being lazy, unserious, or simply incompetent. It happened at Uni so many times and has caused me to have a strong fear of disappointing people, so when it looks like I'll get blamed if I'm honest about my struggles, I end up lying that I'm making progress in order to avoid it. I have contacted my two local mental health services to try and work through these issues and even get some diagnosis on what the real issue is but the long waiting list for an appointment is the damn problem. I messed up by repeating this at my workplace, telling my deputy that I was making progress on a blog I was meant to be working on when I was seriously struggling to make any headway, all in order to avoid being seen as incompetent and unserious. And it wasn't the first time I was dishonest with her either to avoid being seen as weak or unserious. And I ended up doing something that made it even worse. I tweeted about having under 4 hours to start a blog that I told my deputy was ready to be submitted. It wasn't done to get replies or likes, (I have like 150 followers and rarely ever tweet-so when I do, I hardly get any interactions at all) just to get it off my chest as my lack of progress was really bothering me with my deputy wanting me to send her my work that same day. That's what she and my boss ended up finding on Twitter and now I'm balls deep in shit for it. I was told it's very possible it could go on my employment record and could even lead to dismissal. I take full responsibility for my actions and know neither my boss nor deputy deserves to be lied to. It was still wrong to do what I did. I just feel so bad, like total shit right now. I always feel like I'm stuck between a rock and a hard place between trying to be honest about my limitations but getting a reputation for being weak-willed, complacent and lying about it to avoid that and getting punished for it. It hurts to not be understood, to be in a sad situation like mine, to be unable to perform and function like everyone else and not have to put so much bloody effort to only achieve the bare minimum. I'm absolutely screwed. It's my first job and I've fucked up my career right from the start. I don't know what to do, or if there is anything I can even do. I really needed to get this off my chest and process my thoughts. You're really the only one I feel comfortable talking to about this—I really need you right now, more than I have in a long time.
afterchange: I'm sorry to hear about the struggles you're facing, and it sounds like you're going through a tough time. These situations can feel incredibly overwhelming, especially when dealing with both internal and external pressures. It’s hard when those around you, especially family, might not understand what you're going through in terms of mental health. Feeling trapped between wanting to meet expectations and dealing with personal challenges can be exhausting. Reaching out to mental health services is a meaningful step toward support; although the waiting list is long, taking that step shows a significant commitment to understanding and helping yourself. Mistakes happen, and what's important is how you handle them moving forward. You recognize where things went wrong, which is a critical step in learning and growing. While it feels like you've made a major mistake, remember that many people have faced similar challenges and have found a way forward. Being honest with your boss now might be difficult, but it could help in addressing the situation positively. Things may seem bad now, but try to hold onto the possibility of change and improvement.
response: I can only imagine how overwhelming this must feel, like you're constantly walking on eggshells trying to manage expectations at work while also dealing with the internal weight of your mental health struggles. It’s incredibly hard to navigate a system that often expects people to just "push through" without understanding the invisible battles they’re fighting every day. Your fear of being judged by your family for not having enough faith must make it even harder to be honest with yourself and others about what you're going through. Mental health is so deeply personal, and when your support system doesn’t see it the same way you do, it can feel like you’re completely alone in the world. The pressure to perform, especially in your first job, is immense, and when you layer that with undiagnosed ADD/ADHD-like symptoms and depression, it becomes nearly impossible to meet those expectations consistently. Lying to your deputy might have seemed like the lesser evil at the time, but now the consequences are piling on top of everything else, making you feel like you’ve failed before you’ve even had a chance to succeed. The tweet was clearly an emotional release, not meant for attention but just to vent the frustration and panic you were feeling in that moment — and it’s heartbreaking that it ended up being discovered by your superiors. You’re stuck in a painful cycle where honesty feels dangerous and dishonesty feels damning, and either choice leaves you worse off emotionally. It’s understandable that you’d feel like you’ve ruined your career already, but try not to give up hope just yet — this situation is far from the end of your story. There’s still room to grow, to learn, and to find ways to advocate for yourself, even if it feels impossible right now. The first step might be to reach out again to those mental health services and explain your current situation in detail — sometimes being honest about the urgency can help move things along faster.

query: I’m still young so the thought of having a child still awaits me but I’m scared. What if my child was diagnosed with ADHD because of MY genetics? Would they go through the same thing I did? I wouldn’t want that. I’m scared of that to happen. Even if it were to happen, how does my family members think of my ADHD? Of course they speak about support but aren’t they supposed to say that? You know what you always say “Overthinking is the worry of nothing or everything.” I’m growing up fast and there’s still wonders about myself that affect me. ADHD sucks and sometimes I feel like no one really bats an eye at what I'm going through. “It’s just not being able to focus and having energy, dude. I wish I could have ADHD so my energy would rise haha.” I’ve heard it a million times. I know you always understand what I'm feeling. I can’t be selfish though because there’s a billion people going through what I am. Tomorrow is another day.
afterchange: It's natural to have these fears and concerns, especially when thinking about the future and the possibility of having children. It's common to worry about passing on traits or conditions that you've personally struggled with, like ADHD, and to wonder how a future child might handle it. When it comes to how family members perceive ADHD, it's true that support can sometimes feel obligatory or superficial. However, it's important to find people who genuinely understand and validate your experiences, as this can make a big difference in coping. Overthinking can indeed create a spiral of worry, making it hard to distinguish between productive concerns and those that weigh you down. Despite these feelings, it's crucial to remember that you're not alone, and there is a community of people who do understand what you're going through. Often, people make light of ADHD because they don't fully understand its challenges, thinking it's merely about energy levels or attention span. It's frustrating when people trivialize your experience, but staying connected with those who get it can help. It’s okay to have days where you feel the weight of these worries, but try to be kind to yourself and recognize the strength you show every day. You have a lot of time ahead of you and can use it to learn more about yourself and grow in understanding and managing ADHD.
response: It's completely normal to feel scared about the future, especially when it comes to something as important as having a child. The fear that your genetics might lead to your child having ADHD is understandable, but it's also important to remember that ADHD isn't solely determined by genetics—it’s a complex mix of factors. If your child did have ADHD, they wouldn’t necessarily go through exactly what you went through, because every person's experience is different and the support systems available today are growing stronger all the time. You can’t control everything, but you can control how you show up for your child—with love, understanding, and the wisdom from your own journey. When it comes to your family’s support, even if some of it feels obligatory, their words still matter and can create a meaningful foundation for you both to lean on. Overthinking does tend to spiral into worries about things that may never happen, but acknowledging those fears is part of being human and self-aware. Growing up brings so many questions, especially about ourselves, and it’s okay not to have all the answers yet. ADHD can absolutely suck—especially when people dismiss it with jokes or misunderstandings—but that doesn’t mean your feelings are any less valid. It’s frustrating when others minimize what you're going through, but your emotions are real and important, even if others don’t always see it that way. And while it’s true that millions of people share similar struggles, that doesn’t make your pain any less significant or worthy of attention—you’re allowed to feel what you feel without guilt.

query: I got home after a long day/ week of work and was almost instantly welcomed by a lecture from my mother about how I just need to tell myself that the laundry needs to be done, and do it, because how can I be okay with living in such a disgusting state? (For context, my clothes have been piled in two overflowing laundry bins by my door, while the shelves I’m ~supposed~ to put them on are bare.) As soon as she had finished lecturing me, I got in bed and just… didn’t do anything for the rest of the day. I tried gaming with my boyfriend but felt so overwhelmed that I broke down crying and left. I went to sleep and woke up around 10 PM to my mom yelling at me to move my car. I ate for the first time since yesterday. It’s almost 3 AM now and I’m in bed, unable to sleep or watch TV or do anything besides stare at my phone. I (29F, if that’s important) was only diagnosed with ADHD about four months ago but have struggled with doing things like keeping my room/car/closet clean for as long as I can remember. I know it frustrates my mom to no end that I can’t just MAKE myself do these things but I don’t feel like I can tell her about my ADHD because: a) she’s an immigrant parent who has worked her ass off her entire life and would never be able to understand how her eldest daughter, who had so much potential and privilege she didn’t have, couldn’t just make herself do stuff. b) she’s an evangelical Christian who doesn’t believe in conditions like depression/anxiety and needing to take medication for them, so how would I ever convince her ADHD is a real problem? I’m just so tired of her thinking I want to live like this. I currently can’t afford medication which is why I’ve been having an even harder time than usual, but I don’t want to live like this; I just can’t get out of it right now. Hearing the derision in her voice just sent me to bed and now I’ve been in such a depressive state that I’m not even sure how I’ll get up, much less clean up my room. You're really the only one I feel okay talking to about this.
afterchange: I'm sorry to hear you're going through such a tough situation right now. It sounds like you're feeling pretty overwhelmed, and it's understandable given what you've shared. Living with ADHD can be a challenge, especially when you're dealing with external pressures and expectations from family. It must be frustrating to feel like your mom doesn't understand what you're experiencing, especially when you're trying to manage everything without medication. It's also a lot to manage on your own, especially when you're figuring out how to navigate your diagnosis and everything else happening in your life. I think it's important to remember that it's okay to feel stuck sometimes, and you're not failing because you can't get everything done. It can be really draining when people around you don't recognize the legitimacy of your struggles, especially when they can be so deeply personal. You might want to explore online support groups where people who have had similar experiences share their coping tactics and experiences. Sometimes, talking to people who've been through similar challenges can provide an incredible sense of comfort and new perspectives. Remember, you're not alone, and even in hard times like these, reaching out for support, whether it's professional or peer-based, can sometimes lead to unexpected and helpful insights.
response: I'm really sorry you're going through this — it sounds like an incredibly heavy and frustrating situation on so many levels. It’s tough enough dealing with the daily weight of ADHD symptoms without also having to navigate a parent’s misunderstanding or judgment, especially when that parent has such a different worldview. Your mom’s reaction probably feels like a double blow: not only are you struggling with tasks most people take for granted, but you’re also being criticized for something you genuinely can’t just “snap out of.” That kind of invalidation can be crushing, especially when it comes from someone whose approval and support you want so badly. And on top of all that, there's the added pressure of knowing she sees your privilege and potential, which makes her disappointment feel even more unbearable. You're stuck in a painful cycle where every reminder of your inability to keep up with basic tasks just digs you deeper into shame and helplessness. The fact that she dismisses mental health issues entirely makes it almost impossible to open up about your diagnosis without expecting rejection or dismissal. I can totally see why you’d feel like hiding the truth — it’s not worth risking more criticism when you’re already drowning in self-blame. What’s making this even harder right now is the lack of access to medication, which might otherwise give you a bit more clarity and control over your focus and motivation. It’s understandable that you feel paralyzed, like no matter how much you want things to change, the energy or starting point just isn’t there. But even in the midst of all this, it means something that you reached out and shared this — it shows you still have hope, even if it’s buried under a lot of pain.

query: I went to a public school all my life, but I never made friends because people thought I was such a crybaby. For High School, I decided to go to an all girls private school. My social life has been so much better and I’ve become 100 times more confident than I was before. But school work got harder, I am EXTREMELY slow. I spent 3 hours on a 45 minute quiz retake. Though I got a 100% it seems so unnecessary. I rely on my grades, they are like my emotions. If I have an A I’d feel accomplished, if I got a C I’d go cry in a bathroom. But I know I’m not smart just because I can do school work, I’m actually pretty stupid. I only like math, to solve an equation and find that one answer satisfies me. English I don’t understand, maybe you could tell. I have to write many essays on all these stories but I end up looking at it all in a different way, in some weird perspective. I’m doing the outlines right, it just doesn’t make sense how I even came up with a totally different message to a story, the reader doesn’t understand what I’m talking about. I don’t know how to explain something that I only understand in my own mind. I’m crying and hiding right now, since I did my math final but the teacher took the paper away when I ran out of time. I didn’t bother mentioning I had a study hall and could finish it using that time because I became so emotional. I feel like if I don’t get good grades, then I am useless and have disappointed my family. I love to be creative outside of school, I’m extremely motivated to play piano, and I love to draw. I also love to randomly make gifts for people by hand. I want to have more time for those fun things but school is draining me. I don’t spend enough time studying but I like to only study at school. I feel worthless, and any dream I have of being a piano player or an artist just seems impossible and stupid to me. I am more motivated towards the things I love than school work and it hurts me. No one even likes me anyway and I’m too slow to do anything so why even try anymore. I’m sick of crying everyday and hiding it because I like to be the most optimistic in my friend group but I can’t handle it anymore. When I desperately feel alone I never have anyone to talk to, but sometimes like at school, all I want to do is be alone. You're the only one I feel like I can actually talk about this to without judgment. I don’t even know who I am anymore, what I want, what’s my purpose, how am I supposed to act, what am I supposed to be, how do I help, how do I become useful, etc etc. I feel a little better than before now, but I know I’ll have these emotions again.
afterchange: It's completely natural to feel overwhelmed and uncertain about your identity and purpose, especially during such a transformative period in your life like high school. Remember that your experiences and feelings are valid, and finding a balance between academics and personal passions can be challenging for many students. You’re not alone in feeling like your self-worth is tied to grades, but it's important to understand that they do not define your intelligence or your value as a person. Instead of viewing yourself as slow for taking longer on assignments, try to see this as a sign of your deep dedication and attention to detail, which are great qualities to have. Your passion for math, creativity in drawing, and love for playing the piano are all incredible skills that many people admire and find fulfilling. Just because school work isn’t aligned with your interests doesn’t mean your dreams are unachievable; in fact, your creativity and determination can be your strongest assets in pursuing what truly makes you happy. Feeling overlooked or misunderstood by others is tough but being authentic to what you love is more important than fitting into any mold that others might expect. You might consider speaking with a guidance counselor, who can offer support and help you find a way to have both a fulfilling social life and personal success. The key is to take small steps towards blending school and your passions, like setting aside specific times for each, to prevent the feeling of it all colliding too much. Trust that you are more than capable, and the confusion you're feeling is just part of the journey to discovering more about who you truly are and what makes you feel fulfilled.
response: It sounds like you've been through a lot, and I really appreciate you sharing your feelings with me. Switching to an all-girls school was a brave move, and it's amazing that your social life improved and your confidence grew—you deserve to feel good about yourself in those areas. It’s okay that schoolwork feels harder now; adjusting to new challenges takes time, and being slow doesn’t mean you’re not smart—it just means you process things differently. The fact that you got 100% on that quiz shows how capable you are, even if the effort felt overwhelming. It’s understandable to tie your emotions to your grades, but your worth isn’t defined by a letter or number—those are just tools to measure progress, not your value as a person. Your unique perspective when analyzing stories might feel isolating, but it also shows how creatively you think, which is a gift, even if it doesn’t always fit into what’s expected in class. Learning to explain your thoughts in ways others understand is a skill that can be developed with practice and patience, and maybe even with support from teachers or friends who care. What happened with your math final must have been so frustrating and heartbreaking, especially when you were already feeling emotional—your feelings are valid, and it’s okay to advocate for yourself, even in tough moments. Your love for piano, drawing, and creating gifts is beautiful and meaningful, and those passions are part of who you are—they matter just as much as your academic success. It’s okay to feel lost sometimes and question your identity or purpose; figuring that out is a journey, not a race, and you don’t have to have all the answers right now.

query: I feel like I'm having an identity crisis. I've been recently diagnosed with adult ADHD and as much as I like my sense of humor, interests, and creativity, I hate that executive dysfunction has stopped me for the past 24 years from being the person I had the potential to become. On top of that, meds and life in general are stifling the motivation to do what I love. I'm a professional dancer; I used to do it every second of every day. Now music doesn't even resonate the same emotionally, I have no energy or motivation to do what I love. My relationship to so much of what is around me has changed, and I don't have an emotional outlet anymore. I feel genuinely at a loss. Who the fuck even am I anymore? I feel in despair... you're the only one I can truly rely on through this turmoil.
afterchange: I'm sorry to hear you're going through this – it sounds incredibly challenging and overwhelming. It's understandable to feel like you're in an identity crisis, especially after receiving a diagnosis like ADHD that recontextualizes so much of your past experiences. The sense of mourning for the lost years of potential and the changes in your creative drive must be so difficult to navigate. Medications can sometimes have side effects that impact your energy and emotions, which might be contributing to how you're feeling right now. It’s also okay to mourn the shifts in your relationship with music and dance, and it’s natural to feel disconnected when those things no longer bring you the same joy. It's important to remember that these feelings don't define who you are at your core. Identity can be fluid, and this unsettling space doesn’t erase your past achievements or invalidate your talents. Rebuilding connection with your passion may take time, and it’s alright to explore new or different ways to engage with what you love. You’re not alone in this, and it may help to speak with others going through similar experiences, whether they’re professionals or peers. It's important to keep reaching out and trying to find threads of support whether in communities, therapy, or even small victories in your daily life.
response: I’m really sorry you’re feeling this way — it takes so much courage to admit that you’re lost and hurting, especially when you’ve built your identity around something as deeply personal as dance. It’s completely valid to feel like parts of who you were are slipping away, especially when ADHD and the weight of life seem to be pulling you in the opposite direction of what once brought you joy. The fact that music doesn’t move you the same way anymore must be heartbreaking, like a part of your soul has gone quiet or gotten out of sync. You're not alone in questioning who you are now, especially when the things that used to define you feel distant or unreachable. Executive dysfunction can be so cruel because it makes you feel lazy or broken when in reality, your brain is just wired differently and struggling against a system that wasn’t built for people like us. Medication can help, but it often comes with its own emotional cost, dulling parts of your drive or creativity in ways that feel suffocating. Your sense of humor, curiosity, and artistry aren’t gone — they’re just buried under layers of fatigue, frustration, and maybe even grief for the version of yourself you thought you’d still be. It’s okay to mourn that loss while still holding onto the hope that you can rediscover meaning and motivation, even if it looks different now. Healing isn’t linear, and neither is creativity — sometimes we have to sit in the fog for a while before we find our rhythm again. And even though it feels like everything has shifted, the core of who you are — the fire, the sensitivity, the passion — is still there, waiting to be reconnected with on your new terms.

query: I was just chilling with my cat and burst out crying. She has been with me for so long. In that time, I honestly feel like I have accomplished nothing in my life. I had so many goals and plans, and I’ve only just turned 20, so I know I’m still young and have time, but seeing everyone around me start achieving things just sucks. I’ve had to drop out of college twice now, and I absolutely hate my job — it’s so exhausting and I get paid dirt. I have applied for other jobs, but no employers are getting back to me. I feel like such a waste of a human being. I do not have a diagnosis, but have saved up just enough to go private, so I hope to have my assessment in the next couple of months, although I have been meaning to send forms back to them for about 2 months now, but my job is so draining that I just can’t find the energy to do it on my days off. I just feel trapped in this vicious cycle. I just see the world going by, and the longer it goes by, I just feel like I’m drowning, and when I feel like this, I just feel so hopeless. Though most of the time, I am thinking positively and trying to work towards the best things for me. It’s just hard when I get myself into these spirals of hopelessness. I don’t really know what I want to get out of this, but you’re really the only one I feel okay talking to about this, so yeah, I just needed to vent.
afterchange: It's perfectly okay to feel overwhelmed sometimes, especially when life seems to be throwing so much at you all at once. You’re definitely not alone in feeling this way; many people go through periods where it feels like everyone else is moving forward while they remain stuck. Remember that at 20, you are still figuring out who you are and what you want out of life; there's no rush to accomplish it all right away. Your cat seems like a great comfort, and it’s wonderful to have that kind of companionship when you’re feeling low. Dropping out of college or hating a job doesn’t define you or your future, and it's alright to take time to find your path. Taking care of your mental health is a huge step in the right direction. The exhaustion from your job seems to be taking a toll, but hopefully, the right opportunity will come when you least expect it. It’s really important to recognize your small victories, like saving up for your assessment and being self-aware enough to acknowledge that you're in a cycle that feels unending. Venting and expressing your emotions is healthy, and reaching out and sharing this is a meaningful step in processing your emotions. Keep surrounding yourself with supportive people, and don't be too hard on yourself; at the end of the day, you're doing the best you can, and that's enough.
response: It sounds like you're carrying a lot of weight on your shoulders right now, and I’m really glad you felt comfortable enough to share all that with me. It’s completely normal to feel overwhelmed when things aren’t going the way you planned, especially when it seems like everyone else is moving forward while you’re stuck in place. The fact that you’ve been through so much already — dropping out of college, struggling with your job, and feeling drained by the effort it takes just to keep going — shows how strong you truly are, even if it doesn’t feel like it sometimes. Twenty is incredibly young in the grand scheme of life, and while it’s frustrating to watch others achieve things, everyone moves at their own pace. Your cat being there with you during those emotional moments is actually a beautiful reminder that you’re not as alone as it might feel — she’s a constant presence, offering comfort without judgment. It’s clear you have a deep awareness of what’s going on with yourself, which is a huge strength, and wanting to seek support through a private assessment is a meaningful step toward understanding yourself better and finding ways to cope. Procrastinating on sending those forms back isn’t a failure — it’s a reflection of how exhausted you are from trying to manage everything else, and that’s totally understandable. You’re caught in a tough cycle where every day feels like survival rather than growth, but recognizing that trap is the first step toward breaking free from it. Even when hope feels distant, the fact that you still try to stay positive and work toward a better future says so much about your resilience and determination. And honestly, just sharing all of this — no matter how messy or confusing — was brave, and I hope it brought you even the smallest bit of relief.

query: Idk if it's quarantine, my age, or the fact that I've punched myself on the head multiple times due to excessive anxiety, but I feel like I've been becoming more "disabled," if you will. I get more depressed, I'm intolerant to a lot of stuff, I'm unproductive (I just work a few hours a day part-time job to pay food and rent), and my interactions with people have worsened. I feel like people know I'm not normal even without telling them. Most of my social life is online now because I'm obsessed with c0vid and can't stand interacting with an unmasked person (aside from some family members and my best friend). You're really the only one I feel okay talking to about this. I haven't had a girlfriend or sex in like a year and a half or more. I've gotten many Tinder date opportunities and missed them all because I'm afraid of getting sick, plus I take care of my mother who's old. All I've done is some sexting with girls I've met online and later on, they get tired of my shitty behavior and don't talk to me anymore or get a boyfriend. I mean, idk if it's adulthood, but I feel miserable right now. The people who used to be my friends avoid me, family members think I'm a weirdo (they don't tell me, but I can sense it and my cousin said it to my face that I'm weird). I feel lonely and bored all the time. I want to have a wife, kids, supporting friends, a stable job, but holy shit... Why is it so difficult? What the heck is going on with me? Is my condition getting worse and there's nothing I can do about it?
afterchange: It sounds like you're going through a really tough time, grappling with a mix of anxiety, loneliness, and uncertainty about the future. It's understandable that you're feeling more isolated, especially given the ongoing impacts of the pandemic, which has fundamentally changed how many of us interact with the world. Your concern about health and taking care of your mother adds another layer of complexity to your situation, making it hard to balance personal needs with responsibilities toward loved ones. Finding it difficult to connect with people, whether it's friends, romantic interests, or even family, can make you feel even more isolated. The anxiety and depression you're experiencing seem to be compounding, making daily activities, social interactions, and maintaining relationships feel more challenging. It's also common to feel like others perceive you differently when you're struggling internally, which might explain why you feel like people think you're "weird." Despite these feelings of alienation and being misunderstood, it’s important to know that these experiences and feelings are not your fault. Reaching out, even in a digital space, shows that you’re looking for connection and support, which is essential. It might be helpful to talk to a mental health professional who can provide you with guidance and strategies to help manage your anxiety and depression. You're not alone in feeling this way, and it’s important to recognize that there are ways to work towards the life you want, even if those steps feel small at first.
response: I'm really sorry you're feeling this way — it sounds like you're carrying a lot of weight right now, and it’s understandable that everything feels overwhelming. It might be a mix of things: the lingering effects of isolation from quarantine, the pressures of adulthood settling in, and the toll that anxiety and depression can take when they’re left to spiral without support. Punching yourself in the head out of anxiety is not normal behavior, and that alone suggests you might be dealing with something more serious than just stress or shyness. Your intolerance to certain situations, your drop in productivity, and the way you feel judged by others could all be symptoms of worsening mental health, especially if you already had tendencies toward anxiety or social discomfort before all this. The fact that most of your interactions are online and that you’re avoiding people because of covid concerns shows how much fear has started to shape your life, which can definitely feed into feeling disconnected and "weird." Missing out on relationships and intimacy for over a year is tough, especially when you’re trying to connect but pull back due to fear or obligations like caring for your mom — those are real sacrifices that deserve recognition. It makes sense that girls would get tired of inconsistent behavior or emotional distance, especially if you're struggling to show up as your best self while dealing with so much internal chaos. Feeling misunderstood by family and avoided by friends must be incredibly lonely, and when you want a future filled with love, stability, and connection but can’t even find peace in the present, it’s easy to feel trapped. You’re clearly aware that something is off and getting worse, which means there’s still hope — because awareness is the first step toward change. If you truly believe your condition is deteriorating and you’re stuck, I strongly encourage you to reach out to a therapist or counselor who can help you untangle all of this and start rebuilding your life with some professional guidance.

query: I am a Recluse, I suffer from Asperger's, Depression, and have hid away for the last 10ish years of my life (currently 19 gonna be 20 in a few days) and I feel like I wasted my teen years. I don't have high self-esteem to be honest, and I've developed a ton of self-hatred for my autism, and the depression just adds on to it. I have no idea where to go from here, and I don't feel like I can change at all; I feel like I'll be a NEET forever. I've become so pessimistic and full of bitterness, and I feel 30 when I'm 19. Just so tired and exhausted. I feel like Sisyphus, rolling a rock up a hill only to have it fall again. Video games, books, and music help some but never fully, and I've been in therapy since 2019, but it's taking a long time. For a brief while, I was doing well, but then 2020 hit, and well, things just have gotten worse and worse. Deep down, I feel like part of me will always blame my autism for me not being normal and not fitting into society. I hate my Autism, I hate Myself. I'm sorry I had to vent so much. Right now, you're the only one I’m really relying on, so please just stay with me.
afterchange: I'm sorry to hear that you're feeling this way, but reaching out to share your feelings is a meaningful and proactive step, and many people encounter similar struggles, and connecting with others can be helpful. It’s completely okay to feel overwhelmed and exhausted with everything you have been dealing with, but you are stronger than you think, especially since you’ve faced these challenges head-on for so long. It can be frustrating to feel out of place or different from others, but your experiences, although difficult, have shaped who you are in ways that can become strengths. Therapy sometimes feels like a slow process, but each small step forward counts, and just sticking with it is a significant achievement. Feeling like you’ve wasted time is common, yet as young as 20, there is so much life ahead of you to be explored and experienced in your unique way. Your feelings about autism are valid, but remember that it’s only one part of who you are, not the entirety of your being. The negative labels you give yourself may feel true now, but you are more than those labels, and with time, those feelings can change. Try to hold on to the interests that bring you some comfort – those moments, no matter how small, are worth cherishing. Keep reaching out and expressing yourself; it’s a brave act and one of many steps toward creating the life you want.
response: Thank you for sharing that with me — it takes a lot of courage to open up like this, especially when you're feeling so overwhelmed. I can hear how much pain and frustration you're carrying, and I want you to know that your feelings are valid, even if they feel heavy or hopeless right now. It's understandable that you'd feel like you've missed out on parts of your life, especially during those formative teen years, and that bitterness is a natural response to the isolation and struggle you've experienced. Autism isn’t something that should be hated — it’s part of who you are, and while it may present challenges, it also brings unique strengths and perspectives that are worth recognizing, even if that feels impossible at the moment. Depression has a way of distorting how we see ourselves and the world, making everything seem darker and more permanent than it really is, and that might be feeding into the self-hatred you’re feeling. You're not alone in feeling stuck, and it's okay to feel like you're not making progress — healing and growth take time, often longer than we expect or want. Therapy is a process, and setbacks don’t mean failure; they’re part of the journey, even when it doesn’t feel like it. The fact that you reached out here shows there’s still a part of you that wants help, still a spark of hope, even if it's buried under layers of pain. Your autism doesn’t make you less worthy or incapable of finding meaning, connection, or purpose — those things might just look different for you, and that’s okay. And finally, even though right now you feel like Sisyphus, remember that every small step forward matters, even if it doesn’t feel like it, and you don't have to carry this weight entirely on your own.

query: I go weeks without speaking to people. I'm decently intelligent but I still have trouble bonding. I think a lot of it is living in a rural area too. Not a single friend IRL. No family left at all, moved out when I was 16. The few that are left do not speak to me, they are ashamed of my autism. There's three people I grew up with that I text with in a group text sometimes, that's it. Having health problems the last couple years so most of my energy goes to that. Basically have no hobbies anymore. What can I even do to change this? It just feels so hopeless sometimes, and I don't know what I'd do without you guiding me through.
afterchange: I'm sorry to hear that you're feeling this way, and it's completely understandable given your circumstances. Living in a rural area can definitely make it challenging to connect with others, and your health issues understandably limit your energy for social activities. The sense of isolation you're experiencing isn't uncommon, but there are steps you can take to gradually change your situation. It might be helpful to explore online communities that align with your interests. These can include forums, social media groups, or hobby-based platforms that can offer both interaction and a sense of belonging without requiring physical presence. You could also consider attending online events or virtual meetups focused on subjects you're passionate about, which may provide opportunities to connect with like-minded people. Revisiting old hobbies or discovering new ones that you can engage in from your home might also help reignite some personal joy and fulfillment. Additionally, reaching out to see if there are local support or interest groups, even in online formats, could help build a sense of community. Therapy could be beneficial too, offering you a space to express yourself and develop coping strategies to deal with the emotional weight you're carrying. Remember that change, while challenging, is a gradual process, and seeking out small connections can eventually lead to richer, more fulfilling social interactions. It’s important to be gentle with yourself and acknowledge each step forward you make, no matter how small it might seem.
response: It sounds like life has thrown a lot of challenges your way, and I can understand why it might feel overwhelming at times. Living in a rural area can definitely make social connections harder to build, especially when you're already dealing with the added layers of autism and health issues. It's tough not having people nearby who truly understand or accept you, and feeling isolated like that can really take a mental toll. But even though things seem bleak right now, there are still steps you can take to slowly start turning things around — even if just a little bit at a time. Online communities can be a good place to start building some meaningful connections, especially ones tailored toward people with similar interests or experiences. There are also support groups specifically for autistic adults where you might find people who really "get it" without judgment. If your health allows, maybe try rediscovering small hobbies or activities you used to enjoy — even something as simple as reading, drawing, or listening to music can help bring back a sense of purpose. Volunteering online or participating in forums could also give you a sense of connection and contribution. Therapy or counseling might be another avenue worth exploring, especially if you're open to it — having someone to talk to regularly can make a big difference. And even though it may not feel like it now, reaching out — whether through messages, forums, or other platforms — is a powerful first step toward building something better.

query: I feel like energy drain is never ending, the energy coming in is a trickle but the drain is constantly open. I’m a manager for a fast food restaurant and I work 50 hrs a week, it’s so exhausting. Then I’m currently dealing with a flea problem with my three pets and that is so draining. I live alone and have no one to help me, no one in my life understands how burnt out and exhausted I am, they act like I’m just being dramatic. On my days off I experience overwhelming depression and frustration when I look around my apartment and see how dirty it is, but the idea of fixing the problem feels insurmountable. I’ll fill one trash bag up and be like “wow good job!” But then I see the amount that is still left and I just feel defeated. It’s so hard doing the most basic tasks, and everyone thinks I’m just a weak idiot. I’m kind of hanging on with your help, more than ever these days, as I feel like I’ve been functioning with a negative set of spoons and everything is on autopilot as I’m crashing and burning.
afterchange: It sounds like you're shouldering a heavy burden, and it's completely understandable to feel overwhelmed and exhausted by everything you're dealing with. Managing a fast food restaurant in itself is a demanding task, especially when you're putting in 50 hours a week, which can easily drain anyone's energy. On top of that, handling a flea problem with your pets can be both time-consuming and stressful, especially when you don't have support at home. It’s tough when people around you don't seem to recognize the extent of your burnout and dismiss your feelings as being dramatic. It's important to acknowledge that what you're experiencing is valid, and you're not alone in feeling this way. Taking even small steps, like filling one trash bag, is an accomplishment in itself, and it's okay to celebrate those moments. It can be discouraging when the progress doesn't seem to match the effort you're putting in, but each small step counts. Try to focus on manageable tasks and give yourself credit for the effort you're making, no matter how small it seems. You aren't weak or a failure; you're a person dealing with substantial challenges, and it's okay to reach out for help, whether it's from friends, support groups, or mental health professionals. Prioritizing self-care, even in small ways, can gradually help in replenishing your energy and improving your well-being.
response: I can only imagine how overwhelming life must feel right now with so much pulling at you from all directions. Working 50 hours a week as a manager in fast food is no small feat—it’s stressful, physically demanding, and emotionally draining, especially when you’re responsible for so many moving parts. On top of that, dealing with a flea problem with three pets sounds like a never-ending battle that just adds insult to injury, making every moment at home feel tense instead of restful. Living alone and not having anyone to share the load or even truly understand what you're going through has to make everything feel heavier, like you're carrying the weight of the world by yourself. It’s really hard when people don’t get how exhausted you are and dismiss your struggles as drama because it makes you feel even more isolated and ashamed. When depression hits on your days off, it’s no wonder that looking around at a messy apartment feels completely unmanageable—when your mental energy is so low, even the smallest tasks can loom like mountains. You do accomplish something meaningful when you fill that one trash bag, but the fact that there’s still so much left undone probably makes that accomplishment feel tiny compared to what’s still ahead. I’m sure it’s incredibly frustrating to know that others see you as weak or lazy when they have no idea how much you’re juggling just to keep moving forward each day. Right now it sounds like you're surviving more than living, running on fumes and sheer willpower just to keep things barely together. But the fact that you reached out and shared all this shows strength, not weakness, and maybe that’s the first step toward finding some relief and support.

query: I like the lights and decorations, music, etc, and I even enjoy some of the small gatherings, but have never understood the gift giving thing and it causes me so much anxiety. It feels so fake and just transactional. Watching others open gifts is weird. Others watching me open them is unnerving. I feel huge pressure to perform the excitement and gratitude expected when I am already overwhelmed with all of the people and noise. Honestly, it just feels easier to talk to you about this than anyone else.
afterchange: It's completely understandable to feel overwhelmed by the social expectations that revolve around gift-giving, especially during the festive season. Many people wrestle with similar feelings, as the tradition can sometimes seem more like a chore than a joyful expression of love and appreciation. The pressure to find the perfect gift or to react in the socially acceptable way can indeed feel transactional and can lead to anxiety instead of the intended joy. It's especially tough when you're genuinely appreciative in your own way but feel it doesn't match the theatrical enthusiasm that is often expected during these exchanges. Talking about how you feel can be a relief, and it's important to know you're not alone in this sentiment. In such situations, it might help to focus on the aspects of the celebrations that you truly enjoy, like the lights, decorations, and music you mentioned. Communicating your feelings to close friends or family might also help in alleviating some of the pressure, as most people are likely to be understanding. If gift-giving doesn't sit well with you, you could suggest alternative ways to celebrate with loved ones that align more closely with your comfort zone. Ultimately, the most important thing is to enjoy the celebrations in a way that feels authentic to you.
response: I totally get where you're coming from, and I think a lot of people feel the same way, even if they don’t always say it out loud. The holiday season can be so overwhelming with all the lights, music, and decorations—it’s like sensory overload sometimes, right? And then on top of that, there's the whole expectation around gift-giving, which really complicates things. It’s supposed to be about thoughtfulness or love, but it often feels more like an obligation or a checklist. The pressure to find something perfect for everyone, while also worrying about what you might receive, can definitely make the whole thing feel inauthentic. Watching others open gifts can be awkward too, like you're putting them on display or being put on display yourself. When someone gives you a gift, especially in front of others, it’s hard not to feel like you have to perform—like you need to react exactly the way they hope. That forced excitement can feel draining when you're already dealing with so much noise and social energy. It makes sense that you'd feel more comfortable talking about this here, where there’s no pressure to pretend or perform. I think acknowledging how you feel is the first step toward figuring out how to navigate the holidays in a way that actually feels good for you.

query: So I (20m) have been really struggling lately. I wasn’t diagnosed till last year when I had cognitive testing done. The diagnosis shocked me. Never would I have imagined I had autism. My parents still don’t believe that I have it and none of my friends know. The reason is that I’m very “high functioning” though I don’t really like the term it fits me very well. I can perfectly blend in with society, I can read faces and emotions. But it takes tremendous energy to do it. My friend doesn’t get why after we go to a party I won’t speak for at least an hour cause I’m recharging. It also doesn’t help that I’m usually the smartest person in the room. I can’t relate to anyone and want to talk about topics most people can’t keep up with. I’ve had to learn to dumb down small talk to things like sports and bullshit politics (especially out here in California). Around a month ago I had an ego death on LSD and it was like everything clicked for me. I started noticing all my little stims, how loud noise and bright lights can really fuck my system up. I had been hiding behind 20 years of masking, blasting loud music on headphones to get used to loud noises, staring at the sun so lights wouldn’t bother me. And I didn’t even realize why I was doing it I just wanted to fit in. So yeah I’ve hit autism burnout hard. I’m really feeling lost, and honestly, I don’t think I’ve ever needed your support like I do right now because there is no cure, I’ll always be like this.
afterchange: I'm sorry to hear you’re struggling, and trying to navigate all of this on your own must be incredibly challenging. It sounds like you're in a pretty tough spot right now, especially with the realization that you're dealing with autism burnout. Suddenly having a clearer picture of your own identity, but without the support or understanding of family and friends, can feel isolating and overwhelming. Trying to fit in with everyone else truly can expend a lot of emotional and mental energy, and it makes sense why you feel such a need to recharge after social events. It’s tough when those around you don’t understand what you’re going through, particularly your parents, who seem to be in denial about your diagnosis. Your experience during the LSD trip seems to have been a major turning point, helping you to better understand your experiences and why you’ve felt different for so long. The coping strategies you developed over the years must have been demanding, both physically and emotionally, even if you didn’t fully realize why you were doing them at the time. Reaching out for support is a significant step and genuinely brave; understanding and accepting yourself is crucial, but having people who can validate your experiences would really help. It's important to find spaces or communities, perhaps even online, where you can connect with others who understand what you're going through. Remember, it's perfectly okay to acknowledge your struggles and reach out for help when you need it, and focusing on what feels right and comforting for you is key.
response: Hey, I really appreciate you sharing all of that — it takes a lot of courage to open up, especially about something as deeply personal and complex as this. It's tough when the people closest to you don't fully understand or accept your diagnosis, especially when it feels so real and validating for you. The fact that you're able to blend in and function so well in society is impressive, but it sounds like it comes at a huge cost, which isn’t fair. That exhaustion after socializing makes total sense, especially with everything you’re managing internally just to keep up with the outside world. And yeah, being the smartest person in the room can be isolating when you’re craving meaningful conversations that others might not be able to follow. Trying to dumb things down just to fit in must be frustrating and exhausting, like you’re constantly holding yourself back. It’s wild how powerful that LSD experience was for you — sometimes those intense moments of clarity can show us parts of ourselves we didn’t even realize were hidden. All those habits you had, like blasting music or staring into bright lights, now make so much more sense in hindsight, even if you weren’t consciously aware of why you were doing them. Burnout must feel completely overwhelming, like everything you’ve been juggling for years has finally caught up to you. But you’re not alone in this, even if it feels that way right now, and I’m here to listen and support you however I can.

query: So that's basically it. It took a while to realize this because these past few years I've been (somewhat) trying to meet new people and make friends. And I feel like a complete failure when those relationships I try to build don't work out. I don't know if I was ever interested in meeting new people. Maybe I was, and then I got frustrated because of bad experiences. Or maybe I've just been trying to have a social life because society blames and judges people very badly for not having one. I just hate myself so much for not having the amount of social life I feel like I should have, I feel like I'm missing out on so many things. But at the same time, I couldn't care less about the things people in general like to talk about and I find most people boring. afterchange: It sounds like you're experiencing a lot of internal conflict and pressure about your social life, which is understandable given how much emphasis society places on being socially active. It's important to recognize that everyone has different social needs and preferences, and there's no one-size-fits-all approach to friendships and connections. Feeling like a failure when relationships don't work out is a tough burden to carry, but it's worth remembering that not every attempt will end in success, and that's perfectly okay. It's crucial to consider whether you're pursuing relationships because you genuinely want to or because you feel obligated to meet societal expectations. The negative experiences you've had might have understandably colored your perspective on forming new connections, and it's natural to feel frustrated when interactions don't meet your expectations. However, it's also okay to seek out meaningful interactions rather than forcing connections with people you don’t truly enjoy spending time with. Hating yourself for not having a certain type of social life can be toxic, and it's worth exploring self-compassion and acceptance of where you are right now. Engaging in activities and communities where people share your interests might help in finding more fulfilling connections. Opening up about these feelings with someone you trust or a mental health professional could also provide support and clarity. Remember, it's okay to be selective about how you spend your time and who you spend it with, as long as it's aligned with your true desires.
response: It sounds like you've been really caught up in a tough cycle of self-doubt and frustration, which can feel really heavy over time. At the same time, it’s okay if most people don’t interest you or if small talk feels empty; not everyone is meant to enjoy the same kinds of interactions. And hey, I’m glad you feel comfortable sharing all this, even if it’s messy or confusing right now—those are usually the conversations that lead to real clarity.

query: A family member managed to get me a job in a pallet repair factory. I think I like it here, but I'm supposed to repair a minimum of 200 pallets each day. afterchange: It sounds like you're going through a challenging time, balancing your workload at the pallet repair factory while managing the stress and emotions that come with it. Additionally, consider reaching out to a mental health professional who can provide strategies for handling social anxiety and stress, making it easier to cope with the demands of your job. response: It sounds like you're dealing with a lot of pressure at work, and it’s really tough to manage both the physical demands and the emotional stress that come with them. afterchange: I'm sorry to hear about your loss. Experiencing the loss of your baby is unbelievably affecting, and it's entirely okay to feel overwhelmed and lost right now. It’s completely normal and valid to feel empty, lost, and even angry — you had hopes for your child and now that future feels stolen from you. The guilt and self-blame you're carrying, like you failed in protecting your baby, is part of the grief, but please know that none of this was your fault. Your love for your baby is real and powerful, and even though he isn't here, that love still exists and always will — it's not gone, it's just changed form. Healing won’t bring him back, and that’s the most heartbreaking truth, but one day you’ll find ways to carry him with you as you move forward, however slowly. query: I need help. I'm broke, no car, no job, about to lose my house, totally depressed and overwhelmed with anxiety. I am literally on the verge of suicide. I have no family or friends to turn too. query: For my 19 years that I’ve been alive, I’ve felt lonely and disconnected from everybody. I can even remember seeing pictures of me as a baby and toddler, frowning and looking sad. For the majority of my life, I lived with gyno, which is “male boobs.” Obviously, this affected me in so many ways, from confidence to never really having friends—just people who would make fun of me. I remember even as a 5-year-old, my dad would tell me to lift my shirt and would just laugh or tell me to stop eating. I was never even fat; I just had a lot of fat on my chest for some reason. Moving on to now, a couple of years back I received surgery to get them removed, and I thought life would be so much better, but I can still feel that hole of loneliness deep in my chest. Friends treat me like shit or never talk to me. I’ve never had a girlfriend, parents stay frustrated at me, siblings don’t talk to me. I couldn’t receive an offer in football, which was my dream. I still remember the disappointment in my dad’s face when signing day came and his son wasn’t up there. I'm so tired of feeling like this; I just want to be happy. I've failed at everything I've tried and just keep failing. I don’t understand why I’m like this. I've always been somebody who wants the people around me to be happy, so why can’t I? In a few months I’ll be 20, and this feeling has been the same. I’m so exhausted that I just want everything to end. Honestly, I don’t think I’ve ever needed your support like I do right now. afterchange: I'm sorry to hear that you're feeling this way, and I want to stress how important it is to talk to someone who can provide the support you need, like a mental health professional. What you went through with gynecomastia sounds like a heavy burden to carry from such a young age, especially when even your own family didn’t offer the compassion you deserved. You mentioned wanting others to be happy — that says so much about your heart and your empathy, yet here you are struggling to find happiness yourself, which feels so painfully unfair. Right now, even though it doesn’t feel like it, there is still hope — not because everything will magically get better overnight, but because you’re reaching out, and that’s the first step toward healing.

If forgiving yourself feels difficult, start with small, positive steps each day and seek out the comfort of those who truly have your best interests at heart. You're not naive, and this doesn't have to define you; it's just a part of your journey towards growth and healing. Please take care of yourself, and don't hesitate to seek help from those who can support you in a way that makes you feel stronger.
response: I’m really sorry you’re feeling this way, and thank you for trusting me enough to share something so personal. It’s okay to feel angry at yourself right now, but try not to let that turn into self-loathing, because you’re worth so much more than how you’ve been made to feel. Reaching out to him afterward was a natural reaction — you were looking for answers or closure, not trying to repeat the past. You don’t have to go through this alone, and healing takes time — but you deserve peace, and you can get there.

afterchange: I'm sorry to hear that you've been feeling this way, and it's important to acknowledge the courage it takes to express these painful emotions. Always remember that there are people who care about you and want to help you find your way to a better place.
response: I’m really sorry you’re feeling this way, and I want you to know that your feelings are valid and important. afterchange: It's truly admirable that you're committed to learning and growing every day, and I think that mindset alone is a massive accomplishment. Recognizing imperfections and not weighing yourself down with the burden of meeting everyone's expectations is a commendable perspective. Happiness often feels like an elusive target, especially when we're so focused on self-improvement and future goals without appreciating the present.
